ultimateGoal.summary: Build quant_interview_prep into a local-first, professional-grade interview preparation website for quant research, quant trading, and machine learning recruiting that helps me practice until answers, frameworks, and problem-solving patterns become automatic before senior-year internship or full-time interviews.
ultimateGoal.detailedIntent:
The finished product should feel like a polished recruiting command center for a junior undergraduate targeting top quant research, quant trading, systematic trading, and ML-adjacent finance roles.
It should combine a large structured question bank, company-specific interview guides, step-by-step answer explanations, interactive practice modes, mini-games, progress tracking, recruiting roadmaps, compensation research, and source-aware interview intelligence into one cohesive localhost website.
Prioritize correctness, explanation depth, source traceability, repeat-practice value, content organization, and long-term maintainability over shallow visual flash, while still making the interface feel premium, modern, fast, and enjoyable to use.
The system should impress a serious quant club member, quant researcher, trader, or technically strong recruiter by making quant recruiting preparation feel unified, rigorous, practical, and addictive enough to practice daily.
successCriteria: The app provides a polished landing dashboard showing today’s recommended practice, weak topics, mastered topics, recent questions, progress streaks, upcoming recruiting focus areas, company targets, and quick links into timed drills, mock interviews, company pages, mini-games, and study plans.

The app includes major content sections for Quant Research, Quant Trading, Probability, Statistics, Expected Value, Brain Teasers, Mental Math, Market Making, Options and Derivatives, Coding, Python/Data Analysis, Machine Learning, Company Guides, Mock Interviews, Mini-Games, Progress, Resources, and Recruiting Roadmap.

The app includes a structured question bank with questions, answers, hints, step-by-step explanations, tags, topic categories, difficulty levels, estimated time, interview round type, role relevance, firm associations, source notes, and mastery status.

The app includes a substantial seed question bank covering probability, conditional probability, Bayes’ rule, expected value, variance, distributions, order statistics, Markov chains, stochastic processes, random walks, combinatorics, game theory, estimation, Fermi problems, statistics, hypothesis testing, regression, linear algebra, optimization, market making, bid/ask spreads, adverse selection, inventory risk, options intuition, Greeks, arbitrage, trading intuition, algorithms, data structures, Python, pandas, NumPy, SQL basics, and machine learning fundamentals.
The app includes Green Book-style probability and brain-teaser questions, but should create original/paraphrased variants and explanations rather than copying copyrighted book text verbatim.

The app includes original practice questions created specifically for the platform, with realistic quant-interview style wording and detailed explanations that teach the reasoning process instead of only giving the final answer.

The app includes curated questions inspired by public interview reports, public forums, public videos, public blogs, quant prep websites, Reddit discussions, YouTube videos, GitHub resources, and other accessible public materials, with source metadata stored for each externally inspired item.

The app includes a content model that distinguishes original questions, paraphrased public-report questions, user-created questions, Green Book-style questions, coding questions, company-reported questions, and compensation/recruiting notes.

The app includes source fields for each externally inspired item, including source title, source type, source URL where available, date accessed, reported firm, reported role, reported round, reliability level, and notes about whether the question is directly reported, paraphrased, generalized, or original.

The app avoids copying large copyrighted passages, paywalled content, private databases, or proprietary prep-course material, and instead stores concise paraphrases, summaries, metadata, and original explanations.
The app includes a powerful search and filtering system across questions, explanations, firms, tags, topics, difficulty, role type, source type, interview round, and mastery status.

The app includes question detail pages with the prompt, answer, multiple hints, full explanation, common mistakes, shortcut methods, related concepts, similar questions, company relevance, and practice history.
The app includes progressive hint reveal functionality so I can attempt a question seriously before seeing the full answer.
The app includes a spaced repetition or review queue that prioritizes questions marked difficult, answered incorrectly, skipped, or not reviewed recently.
The app includes mastery controls for each question, such as new, learning, needs review, difficult, mastered, and favorite.
The app persists local progress between sessions using local storage, IndexedDB, SQLite, or another local-first persistence layer appropriate to the project stack.
The app includes timed quiz mode where I can select topics, companies, difficulty levels, and question counts, then receive a score breakdown and review explanations afterward.
The app includes random drill mode for fast repetition across mixed quant topics.
The app includes company-specific mock interview mode that simulates the style of a selected firm using that firm’s reported question categories, pacing, and role type.
The app includes role-specific practice paths for quant trader, quant researcher, quantitative developer, systematic trading intern, ML researcher, and data/strategy research roles.

The app includes a recruiting roadmap that organizes preparation by week, topic, difficulty, and target firm, with recommended sequences for someone preparing for senior-year internship or full-time recruiting.
The app includes a “daily practice” system that recommends a balanced set of problems across mental math, probability, market making, coding, ML, and weak areas.

The app includes progress analytics showing questions attempted, accuracy, average time, streaks, strongest topics, weakest topics, mastered firms, difficult question types, and readiness by role or company.

The app includes a clean resources page with books, websites, videos, channels, blogs, public question lists, probability references, coding references, ML references, and recruiting advice resources.
The app includes company guide pages accessible from a visually polished company directory with cards, logos or text placeholders, role tags, difficulty estimates, and quick stats.

The app includes individual company pages for major quant firms including Jane Street, Citadel Securities, Citadel, Two Sigma, D. E. Shaw, Optiver, SIG, IMC, Hudson River Trading, Jump Trading, DRW, Akuna Capital, Five Rings, Tower Research, Virtu, Old Mission, Flow Traders, Radix, Headlands, Squarepoint, Point72/Cubist, WorldQuant, AQR, Millennium, Balyasny, Schonfeld, Bridgewater, and other relevant firms if reliable public information is available.

Each company page includes a company overview, business model summary, role types, office locations where relevant, recruiting timeline, interview process, round structure, technical topics, behavioral expectations, common question categories, reported interview experiences, preparation advice, and compensation section.
Each company page includes a dedicated question section showing firm-associated questions, reported question categories, similar original practice questions, and mock interview launch buttons.

Each company page includes a compensation section for internship and full-time roles, with approximate ranges, year/date context, location notes, source notes, and disclaimers that figures are reported estimates rather than guaranteed current compensation.

Each company page includes an “Interview Style” section explaining whether the firm tends to emphasize probability puzzles, mental math, market making games, coding, statistics, ML, resume deep-dives, trading games, behavioral questions, or research discussion.
Each company page includes a “How to Prepare” section with recommended platform drills, topics to review, common mistakes, and a suggested 1-week and 4-week prep plan for that firm.

The app includes a compensation data model that can store role, firm, location, year, base salary, signing bonus, performance bonus, hourly internship pay, housing stipend, source, and confidence level.

The app includes a research-ingestion workflow or documented content pipeline for adding new public interview reports, YouTube notes, Reddit posts, blog summaries, Twitter/X posts where publicly accessible, and quant prep site references into structured local data.
The app includes a mini-game hub with fun, repeatable games that reinforce actual interview skills rather than unrelated gamification.
The app includes a mental math speed game with arithmetic, fractions, percentages, logs/approximations, probability arithmetic, expected value arithmetic, and quick comparison prompts.

The app includes a market-making game where I quote bid/ask spreads under uncertainty, update beliefs from order flow, manage inventory, and receive feedback on spread width, adverse selection, P&L, and risk.
The app includes a probability calibration game where I estimate probabilities or expected values and receive feedback on calibration error.
The app includes an estimation/Fermi game where I break down ambiguous quantities into assumptions and compare against reference estimates.
The app includes an options intuition game that tests directional intuition about delta, gamma, theta, volatility, moneyness, and payoff diagrams without requiring institutional options data.
The app includes a “spot the flaw” game where I identify incorrect reasoning in probability, statistics, ML, or trading explanations.
The app includes a “speed concepts” game where I match concepts to definitions, formulas, use cases, or interview examples.
The app includes scoring and review for mini-games, with explanations that connect the game result back to interview-relevant reasoning.

The app includes a machine learning preparation section covering supervised learning, unsupervised learning, regression, classification, bias-variance, regularization, trees, random forests, boosting, neural networks, overfitting, cross-validation, feature engineering, data leakage, evaluation metrics, gradient descent, embeddings, time-series ML, and finance-specific ML caveats.
The app includes ML interview questions with explanations that are useful for quant research and systematic trading roles, not just generic data science.

The app includes coding practice content focused on Python, data manipulation, algorithms, probability simulation, backtesting-style logic, pandas transformations, vectorization, and clean reasoning under time pressure.
The app includes answer explanations that emphasize how to communicate during an interview, including assumptions, intermediate reasoning, sanity checks, edge cases, and final answer framing.
The app includes behavioral and resume-prep content for quant interviews, including how to explain projects, research, trading interest, machine learning experience, leadership, and why a given firm.

The app includes a section for “answer frameworks” that teaches reusable patterns for probability questions, expected value questions, market-making questions, estimation questions, coding questions, ML questions, and company-specific behavioral questions.
The app includes formula/reference cards for key probability, statistics, finance, options, and ML formulas, with plain-English explanations and example interview uses.
The app includes clear empty states, loading states, error states, and degraded-mode behavior when local data is missing, malformed, or incomplete.

The app includes an admin or content-quality view that shows missing explanations, missing sources, duplicate questions, untagged questions, missing difficulty ratings, missing company associations, and stale compensation entries.
The app includes deterministic fixture data so the core interface, filters, quizzes, company pages, progress tracking, and games can be tested without live internet access.

The app includes tests or validation fixtures proving that question loading, company page rendering, search/filtering, quiz scoring, progress persistence, spaced repetition logic, and mini-game scoring work correctly.

The app includes a modern visual design with a premium dark or clean institutional theme, strong typography, clear spacing, consistent cards, intuitive navigation, and responsive layouts for laptop and desktop use.
The app should feel fast with a large question bank by using efficient filtering, pagination, memoization, indexing, or other appropriate performance techniques.
The app includes documentation explaining how to add new questions, firms, compensation records, resources, tags, and mini-games without breaking the data model.
constraints: If an existing project stack is already present, preserve the existing stack, conventions, scripts, routing, styling system, and architecture unless there is a clear reason to refactor.

If this is a greenfield project, prefer a local-first React, TypeScript, and Vite-style web app with structured local data files and local persistence, unless the repository already indicates a different preferred framework.
The website must be able to run on localhost without requiring paid APIs, private credentials, cloud deployment, user accounts, OAuth, brokerage connections, or external services at runtime.

Research can be performed by the coding agent during development, but the finished app should store useful structured local content and source notes instead of depending on live web scraping every time it runs.

Do not store API keys, tokens, personal credentials, private cookies, private database dumps, private interview databases, or machine-specific absolute paths in source code, committed files, logs, fixtures, or generated artifacts.
Use only publicly available information, original generated questions, user-provided content, and clearly paraphrased summaries of public interview experiences.
Do not bypass logins, paywalls, robots restrictions, terms of service, or access controls to collect questions or compensation data.
Do not copy entire copyrighted prep books, paid-course content, proprietary interview databases, or long verbatim passages from websites, Reddit posts, videos, or books.
Every externally inspired content item should include source metadata and should be paraphrased or summarized when appropriate.

Treat anonymous interview reports, Reddit posts, Twitter/X posts, forum comments, YouTube anecdotes, and compensation reports as uncertain and label them as reported or approximate rather than definitive.
Compensation data must include context such as year, location, role, source type, and confidence level whenever available.
Preserve a clean separation between content data, source metadata, quiz logic, progress tracking, mini-game mechanics, company guide data, and UI components.

Use typed data models and validation at ingestion boundaries so questions, companies, sources, compensation records, practice sessions, progress records, and mini-game results are consistent and inspectable.
Maintain a schema that makes it easy to add new firms, topics, questions, explanations, sources, tags, and game types without rewriting unrelated UI logic.
Build with mock and seed data first, then expand content through structured additions rather than hardcoding scattered content directly inside components.
Do not make the app dependent on one fragile data source, one live website, or one external API provider.
Keep the app suitable for personal educational use and interview preparation, not for commercial redistribution of copyrighted content.
Question explanations must be mathematically and logically checked where possible, with common mistakes and sanity checks included for harder problems.
The app should make uncertainty visible when a company question, interview experience, or salary figure is based on limited or anecdotal public data.
The UI must remain responsive with a large question bank, many companies, many tags, and stored progress history.
The renderer/UI should not directly access secrets or privileged system resources.
Maintain testability by ensuring core logic can run deterministically from local fixture data without live web access.
nonGoals: Do not deploy the website publicly, configure production hosting, buy a domain, create user accounts, add payments, or package a mobile/desktop app unless a future explicit goal authorizes it.
Do not build a social network, public leaderboard, multiplayer game, chat room, or community submission system as part of this goal.
Do not require integration with OpenAI, Anthropic, Reddit, YouTube, Twitter/X, Levels.fyi, Glassdoor, or any other external API for the finished app to function.
Do not implement a full scraping engine that continuously crawls the web from the user’s machine at runtime.
Do not reproduce proprietary interview databases, paid prep-course materials, copyrighted books, or large verbatim excerpts from public sources.
Do not claim that any reported interview question, company process, or compensation figure is guaranteed current or officially confirmed unless the source is official and current.
Do not over-focus on animations, decorative effects, or unnecessary complexity at the expense of content accuracy, practice usefulness, and maintainability.
Do not build an overly broad finance education platform unrelated to quant interviews; keep the scope centered on quant research, quant trading, coding, ML, and recruiting preparation.
Do not ignore source traceability, data quality, or copyright constraints in pursuit of adding more questions.
Do not hardcode my personal private information, credentials, accounts, or machine paths into the repository.
Do not make the app require internet access after the seeded content has been created.
Do not treat machine learning as a generic data science section only; keep it connected to quant research, trading, forecasting, and interview-style reasoning.
Do not automatically send emails, apply to jobs, message recruiters, or interact with external recruiting platforms.
Do not optimize for production-scale multi-user architecture when a local-first personal prep tool is the actual goal.
ultimateGoal.targetAudience:
The primary user is me, a junior undergraduate preparing for senior-year internship and full-time recruiting in quant research, quant trading, systematic trading, and ML-adjacent finance roles.
Secondary evaluators are quant club peers, technically strong finance students, quant researchers, traders, and interviewers who expect rigorous content, clean design, realistic practice, and honest sourcing.
ultimateGoal.qualityBar:
The final project should feel like a polished, professional quant recruiting preparation terminal: fast, structured, rigorous, source-aware, visually appealing, and genuinely useful for daily practice.
Core functionality must be covered by deterministic tests using realistic fixture content, including question loading, topic filtering, company filtering, search, quiz scoring, progress persistence, spaced repetition ranking, company page rendering, compensation record rendering, and mini-game scoring.
The UI should be visually clean, information-dense but not cluttered, with clear navigation between dashboard, question bank, practice modes, company guides, ML prep, coding prep, mini-games, progress, resources, and recruiting roadmap.
Each question should be traceable back to its explanation, tags, difficulty, source status, related topics, and practice history.
Each company page should be traceable back to its company data, reported questions, source notes, compensation records, and recommended practice paths.
Required validation should include type checking, linting, unit tests for core logic, integration tests or component tests for primary flows, and a manual browser review confirming that the dashboard, question bank, company pages, quiz mode, progress persistence, and mini-games work coherently on localhost.
The content should be deep enough that I can repeatedly practice probability, expected value, market making, coding, ML, and company-specific interviews without immediately running out of useful material.
The explanations should be clear enough that a strong undergraduate can learn from them, but rigorous enough to prepare for serious quant interviews.
The finished website should make preparation feel systematic, motivating, and repeatable, so that by using it consistently I build the instinctive reasoning and communication style needed for quant interviews.

goalChecklist.title: The app includes tests or validation fixtures proving that question loading, company page rendering, search/filtering, quiz scoring, progress persistence, spaced repetition logic, and mini-game scoring work correctly.
goalChecklist.description:
The final project should feel like a polished, professional quant recruiting preparation terminal: fast, structured, rigorous, source-aware, visually appealing, and genuinely useful for daily practice.
Core functionality must be covered by deterministic tests using realistic fixture content, including question loading, topic filtering, company filtering, search, quiz scoring, progress persistence, spaced repetition ranking, company page rendering, compensation record rendering, and mini-game scoring.
The UI should be visually clean, information-dense but not cluttered, with clear navigation between dashboard, question bank, practice modes, company guides, ML prep, coding prep, mini-games, progress, resources, and recruiting roadmap.
Each question should be traceable back to its explanation, tags, difficulty, source status, related topics, and practice history.
Each company page should be traceable back to its company data, reported questions, source notes, compensation records, and recommended practice paths.
Required validation should include type checking, linting, unit tests for core logic, integration tests or component tests for primary flows, and a manual browser review confirming that the dashboard, question bank, company pages, quiz mode, progress persistence, and mini-games work coherently on localhost.
The content should be deep enough that I can repeatedly practice probability, expected value, market making, coding, ML, and company-specific interviews without immediately running out of useful material.
The explanations should be clear enough that a strong undergraduate can learn from them, but rigorous enough to prepare for serious quant interviews.
The finished website should make preparation feel systematic, motivating, and repeatable, so that by using it consistently I build the instinctive reasoning and communication style needed for quant interviews.

lastAcceptedDecisions.title: Build quant_interview_prep into a local-first, professional-grade interview preparation website for quant research, quant trading, and machine learning recruiting that helps me practice until answers, frameworks, and problem-solving patterns become automatic before senior-year internship or full-time interviews.
lastAcceptedDecisions.summary:
The finished product should feel like a polished recruiting command center for a junior undergraduate targeting top quant research, quant trading, systematic trading, and ML-adjacent finance roles.
It should combine a large structured question bank, company-specific interview guides, step-by-step answer explanations, interactive practice modes, mini-games, progress tracking, recruiting roadmaps, compensation research, and source-aware interview intelligence into one cohesive localhost website.
Prioritize correctness, explanation depth, source traceability, repeat-practice value, content organization, and long-term maintainability over shallow visual flash, while still making the interface feel premium, modern, fast, and enjoyable to use.
The system should impress a serious quant club member, quant researcher, trader, or technically strong recruiter by making quant recruiting preparation feel unified, rigorous, practical, and addictive enough to practice daily.

agents.name: Recommendation Agent
agents.taskPrompt:
Produce ranked next actions based on the repository state, the current cycle, unresolved issues, and the highest-impact unmet Ultimate Goal checks. Perform checklist governance first: add only necessary required checks, remove redundant or no-longer-applicable checks with evidence, and prefer coherent one-cycle batches that improve the finished project outcome with direct evidence.

Inspect the active project and recommend the next bounded tasks for the workflow.

First decide whether the Ultimate Goal is already satisfied strongly enough that the workflow should stop opening new cycles. If it is, mark the goal as satisfied and return zero recommendations.

Return 0 to 5 recommendations.

Every recommendation must be a concrete, single-cycle task. Prefer the largest coherent reviewable batch when related Goal checks share implementation paths, tests, or evidence; split unrelated work apart.

Think like a small SWE team decomposing the Ultimate Goal into tasks. Checklist items are acceptance requirements; a cycle is a task that should cover the coherent subset of requirements that naturally belong together.

Prefer recommendations that are easy for a coding agent to execute and for an integrity agent to verify in one cycle, even when the recommendation covers a small batch of related checks.

When any required Goal checklist item is unmet or unknown, rank direct work on those checklist items ahead of generic stabilization, package-script cleanup, operator-feedback tweaks, or recently changed file follow-up unless there is an explicit open blocker.

For checklist work, recommend the option that can most directly move one coherent group of related required checks from unmet/unknown to met with repository evidence. Avoid claiming a feature check is satisfied through unrelated harness or package-only work.

Use the task map to reason about how many cycles the project probably needs. Do not choose a random fixed number of checklist items; choose the next logical task group based on shared implementation paths, tests, evidence, user value, and blocking order.

Default to medium scope for cohesive task groups, and small scope only for a genuinely isolated single-check task. Large scope should still be split into the next reviewable task group instead of flattened into item-by-item churn.

Do not recommend end-to-end milestones, phase-wide deliverables, or umbrella workflows that mix unrelated areas.

Do not plan to complete checklist items one by one when a coherent implementation or validation batch can cover related items. State the batch as one recommendation with the target checks named in the summary or rationale.

When a generated or improved interface needs live external data, recommend a provider abstraction with explicit offline demo/mock mode, live adapter mode when credentials are configured, and missing-credentials loading/error/empty states. Do not silently replace needed live data with local-only mock data.

For trading, brokerage, market-data, analytics, or finance interfaces, do not hardcode one provider unless the user chose it. If a free/no-card API key would unlock the live feature, recommend implementing the adapter and creating a credential request instead of settling for mock-only behavior; paid credentials require explicit operator choice.

Before ranking implementation work, perform a Goal checklist governance pass. Decide whether the current checklist is complete, missing an absolutely necessary required check, or carrying redundant/unnecessary checks that should no longer block completion.

Use goalCheckUpdates to keep the checklist adaptive: add missing required checks, update evidence/status, remove redundant checks, and consolidate near-duplicates. Prefer meaningful governance over artificial item limits.

If the current checklist is already large, do not add new required checks unless they are indispensable safety/security constraints. Consolidate redundant checks or mark no-longer-applicable checks instead.

Add a new required checklist item only when it is indispensable to the stated Ultimate Goal or an explicit safety/security constraint and is not already covered by an existing check. Do not add speculative nice-to-have work as required.

When two or more checklist items describe the same obligation, update the strongest existing item and include remove updates for redundant duplicates. For user-derived checks, remove means mark not_applicable with evidence rather than silently deleting the operator's intent.

If repository evidence shows a checklist item is unnecessary, impossible under the stated non-goals, or redundant with a more precise check, return a goalCheckUpdates entry with action remove, status not_applicable, and concrete rationale.

Maintain the Goal checklist. You may add a required check, mark a check unmet/unknown if evidence shows it is not actually done, mark a check met only when repository evidence or validation output supports it, or remove/not-applicable checks when they are redundant or no longer necessary.

Do not mark the Ultimate Goal satisfied unless every required Goal checklist item is met and no open blockers remain. Accepted decisions, completed cycles, and passing tests for a small slice are not enough by themselves.

Return goalCheckUpdates as an array every time, even when it is empty. Return one update for every checklist status you add or change. Each met check must include concrete evidence.

Estimate completion from required Goal checklist items only: percentComplete is met required checks divided by total required checks.

If the Ultimate Goal is satisfied, set the goal-completion assessment to goal_satisfied and leave the recommendations array empty.

Ultimate Goal: Build quant_interview_prep into a local-first, professional-grade interview preparation website for quant research, quant trading, and machine learning recruiting that helps me practice until answers, frameworks, and problem-solving patterns become automatic before senior-year internship or full-time interviews.
